definition: Expressed in few words; brief and abrupt in speech or writing.
example: The CEO's terse reply — 'No.' — left the board in stunned silence.
mnemonic: TERSE sounds like 'tersely trimmed' — trim every extra word away.
confusableNote: terse vs. concise: both are brief, but terse often implies brusqueness or even rudeness, whereas concise simply means efficient.

definition: A formal, systematic written work that examines a subject thoroughly.
example: Darwin's treatise on natural selection transformed the field of biology.
mnemonic: A TREATISE is a 'treatment' of a topic — thorough and formal.

definition: Vigorous, incisive, and sharply effective in expression or argument.
example: The critic offered a trenchant analysis that exposed every flaw in the policy.
mnemonic: TRENCH + ANT — a sharp trench cuts deep, just like trenchant criticism.

definition: Using far more words than necessary; excessively wordy.
example: The verbose professor took forty minutes to explain what could have been said in five.
mnemonic: VERB + OSE — so full of words (verbs/nouns) that it overflows.

definition: Expressing opinions loudly and forcefully; clamorous and insistent.
example: Vociferous protesters outside the courthouse drowned out traffic noise for blocks.
mnemonic: VOCI (voice) + FEROUS (bearing) — loudly carrying one's voice everywhere.
confusableNote: vociferous vs. verbose: vociferous stresses loudness/forcefulness; verbose stresses excessive wordiness.

definition: Talking fluently and at great length; marked by a ready flow of speech.
example: The voluble tour guide narrated the entire history of Rome without pausing once.
mnemonic: VOLUBLE sounds like 'volume' — always turned up to high volume.

definition: Regularly found and persistently established within a particular area or group.
example: Corruption had become endemic to the region's political institutions over decades.
mnemonic: EN + DEMOS (people) — native to and constant among a specific people or place.
confusableNote: endemic vs. epidemic: endemic means consistently present in a region; epidemic means suddenly spreading widely.

definition: Firmly established and difficult to change, as if dug into defensive positions.
example: Entrenched attitudes about gender roles proved stubbornly resistant to reform.
mnemonic: Like soldiers in a TRENCH — deeply dug in and hard to dislodge.

definition: Lasting only a very short time; transitory.
example: Social media fame is often ephemeral, fading within days of a viral moment.
mnemonic: EPHEMERA = things lasting only a day (Greek hemera = day); think mayfly, living one day.

definition: Occurring in irregular bursts; not steady or continuous.
example: She managed only fitful sleep, waking every hour through the stormy night.
mnemonic: FIT + FUL — coming in fits and starts, not smoothly.

definition: To be an advance warning or sign of something bad; to predict evil.
example: Dark clouds on the horizon seemed to forebode a disastrous voyage ahead.
mnemonic: FORE (before) + BODE (omen) — boding something bad before it happens.

definition: Able to be anticipated or predicted within a reasonable future horizon.
example: Climate scientists warn that sea levels will continue rising for the foreseeable future.
mnemonic: FORE (ahead) + SEE — what you can see coming ahead of time.

definition: A person or thing that signals or foreshadows what is to come.
example: The first robin of spring is a beloved harbinger of warmer weather.
mnemonic: A HARBINGER finds the HARBOR first — a scout announcing what arrives next.

definition: About to happen very soon; impending.
example: With the hurricane imminent, residents were ordered to evacuate the coast.
mnemonic: IMMINENT — think 'I'm in it soon'; it's right on top of you.
confusableNote: imminent vs. eminent: imminent = about to happen; eminent = distinguished or prominent.

definition: Continuing without interruption; never ceasing.
example: The incessant noise from the construction site made concentration impossible.
mnemonic: IN + CESSANT (cease) — in-capable of ceasing; never stops.

definition: Deeply embedded in one's nature or habits; firmly established over time.
example: His ingrained distrust of authority made him question every rule he encountered.
mnemonic: IN the GRAIN of wood — a pattern running so deep it can't be sanded away.

definition: Seemingly endless; tediously long without any apparent end.
example: The interminable board meeting stretched past midnight with no resolution in sight.
mnemonic: IN + TERMINAL — no terminal (ending) in sight; goes on forever.

definition: Occurring at irregular intervals; not continuous or steady.
example: Intermittent rain showers forced the outdoor concert to pause several times.
mnemonic: INTER (between) + MITTENT (sending) — sending signals between gaps, not constantly.

definition: Lasting only a very brief moment; extremely short-lived.
example: A momentary lapse in concentration cost the chess grandmaster the match.
mnemonic: MOMENT + ARY — lasting only a single moment.
confusableNote: momentary vs. momentous: momentary = very brief; momentous = of great importance.

definition: Giving the impression that something bad or unpleasant is about to happen.
example: An ominous silence fell over the crowd just before the verdict was read.
mnemonic: OMEN + OUS — full of bad omens; the omen is baked right into the word.

definition: Lasting or recurring through many years; enduring and perpetual.
example: Budget shortfalls are a perennial problem for underfunded school districts.
mnemonic: PER (through) + ANNUAL — going through year after year without stopping.

definition: To cause something to continue or prevail indefinitely.
example: Stereotypes perpetuate inequality by shaping how people are judged before they speak.
mnemonic: PERPETU(AL) + ATE — making something perpetual by acting to keep it going.

definition: Spreading through and present throughout every part of something.
example: A pervasive sense of distrust made cooperation between the departments impossible.
mnemonic: PER (through) + VASIVE (invade) — invading through every corner.

definition: To be a sign or warning of something, especially something momentous or calamitous.
example: Economists argued that the market volatility portended a severe recession.
mnemonic: PORT (carry forward) + END — carrying a sign of an ending or event ahead.
confusableNote: portend vs. portent: portend is the verb; portent is the noun (a sign or omen).

definition: To be an early indicator or symbolic representation of something that will come later.
example: Many scholars argue that Hamlet's soliloquies prefigure modern psychological introspection.
mnemonic: PRE (before) + FIGURE — sketching out the figure of what comes before it arrives.

definition: To plan or think out an action, especially a crime, beforehand.
example: The prosecution argued that the defendant had premeditated the attack weeks in advance.
mnemonic: PRE (before) + MEDITATE — meditating on your plan before you carry it out.

definition: To be a sign or omen of a future event; to predict.
example: The sudden withdrawal of ambassadors presaged the outbreak of war.
mnemonic: PRE (before) + SAGE (wise prophet) — a wise sign coming before the event.
confusableNote: presage can be both a verb and a noun; as a noun it means an omen or warning sign.

definition: Having or showing accurate knowledge of events before they occur.
example: Her prescient warnings about the housing bubble went unheeded until the crash.
mnemonic: PRE (before) + SCIENT (knowing, as in science) — knowing things before others do.

definition: Accurately predicting or foreshadowing future events.
example: In hindsight, the author's prophetic novel described the rise of surveillance states with eerie precision.
mnemonic: PROPHET + IC — having the quality of a prophet who sees the future.

definition: Breaking out again after a period of dormancy; experiencing renewed activity or virulence.
example: A recrudescent outbreak of the disease alarmed health officials who had declared it eradicated.
mnemonic: RE (again) + CRUDE (raw, unhealed) — a raw wound breaking open again.

definition: Occurring at irregular and infrequent intervals; scattered.
example: Sporadic gunfire was heard throughout the city but never sustained long enough to indicate a battle.
mnemonic: SPORADIC — like spores scattered randomly; no pattern, no regularity.

definition: Relating to worldly time rather than eternity; not permanent.
example: The monk renounced temporal pleasures to devote himself entirely to spiritual pursuits.
mnemonic: TEMPO (time) + RAL — of or relating to time; thus subject to passing away.
confusableNote: temporal vs. temporary: temporal relates to time or worldly existence; temporary simply means not lasting long.

definition: Lasting only for a short time; passing through without staying permanently.
example: The city's transient population made it difficult to establish stable community organizations.
mnemonic: TRANSIT + ENT — always in transit, never staying; just passing through.

definition: Not lasting; existing only briefly before passing away.
example: The politician's popularity proved transitory, collapsing within months of the scandal.
mnemonic: TRANSITORY — everything is in TRANSITION; nothing stays fixed.
confusableNote: transitory vs. transient: both mean short-lived, but transient often emphasizes physical movement through a place; transitory emphasizes the brevity of duration.

definition: Not well known, discovered, or clearly expressed; hard to perceive or understand.
example: The professor's lecture drew on obscure medieval texts that few students had ever encountered.
mnemonic: OB (over) + SCURE (dark) — darkness cast over it, making it hard to see or find.

definition: To use deception to deprive someone of money or possessions; to cheat.
example: The con artist swindled elderly investors out of their retirement savings.
mnemonic: SWINDLE sounds like 'spin a lie' — spin a web of lies to steal.
